topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting irritation. I stared at the quarterly reports, my jaw tight. It was impossible. Sarah, the new analyst, had just presented the figures for the Q3 project, and the compensation breakdown was… jarring. I leaned back in my chair, the leather squeaking in protest, and let out a slow, measured breath. The numbers showed a significant discrepancy; Sarah was earning nearly double what I was making for a role that, frankly, I felt I was performing at a superior level. A tight knot formed in my stomach, a cold, unwelcome clench. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and began meticulously re-reading the salary structure, searching for some hidden error, some oversight that would validate my own position. The air around me felt thick, heavy with unspoken judgment.

[story 2]

"Let me see those projections again, Sarah," I said, my voice clipped, deliberately flat. I watched her nervously fiddle with her tablet, a faint flush creeping up her neck. When she finally presented the data, the figures were laid bare. My eyes scanned the compensation section, and a strange, unpleasant tightening spread across my chest. It was obvious. The gap between my salary and hers wasn't just a difference in title; it was a chasm. I shifted in my seat, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, a defensive posture I didn't even realize I was adopting. A subtle narrowing of my eyes was enough; I let the silence stretch, letting the weight of the inequality settle between us. I wanted to say something sharp, something to immediately correct the imbalance, but the words caught, lodging somewhere behind my teeth.

[story 3]

I watched Sarah pack up her things after the meeting, her movements quick and almost furtive. As she walked toward the exit, I felt a distinct hardening in my expression. It was a slow, deliberate hardening, like stone settling into place. The realization of the financial disparity was a dull, persistent ache beneath my ribs. I stood up, pushing my chair back with a deliberate scrape against the floor. I didn't look at her as she reached the door; instead, I focused on straightening a stack of files on my desk, smoothing out every crease with unnecessary force. Every movement was precise, controlled, conveying a clear sense of internal disapproval that I made sure she couldn't misinterpret. The atmosphere in the room seemed to shrink around me, isolating my reaction.
